After getting a basic concept of databases in the previous post, of course we have to enter the database chapter now.
What is MongoDB? MongoDB is the NoSQL database we covered in the previous post. I also mentioned NoSQL's data format is similar to JSON.
MongoDB's actual data format is BSON, not JSON. BSON stands for Binary JSON — a binary representation. So it's more space-efficient than JSON and supports more data types.
So BSON is also called a superset of JSON.
That covers the key points and differences about MongoDB. Now let's see how to use MongoDB.
I won't cover local MongoDB installation here — if you want to run it locally, I'm sure you can find tutorials online. Instead, I'll use MongoDB Atlas.
If you really can't find a local install guide, see my earlier post
(1) Frontend dev forced to eat mango - Getting to know MongoDB and installing MongoDB (article in Mandarin)
. Unless someone wishes for me to write a local install post, I probably won't write it XD.

What's MongoDB Atlas? MongoDB Atlas is the official cloud service from MongoDB. You can build a MongoDB database on it and access it via network connection. In practice, putting databases on cloud platforms is more common, but this isn't absolute — depends on project needs.
Click this link to go to the MongoDB website and register an account:
I'd recommend using a GitHub account to register.
After all, as an engineer, you should have a GitHub account, right?
After clicking register, you should see the privacy policy page — just agree, and you'll get to:
Wait a moment on this screen. Then it'll ask you a few questions. Here's my answer for reference:
What type of application are you building?
Of course you can pick your own — I'm just providing a reference. Then you'll go to the plan selection screen (of course we pick Free):
Next is the Security Quickstart screen, where you create a user that can access the database. I'll create one called
and let it generate a random password.
Save this username and password — we'll need them later.
After clicking Create User, you'll jump to "Where would you like to connect from?". This is asking where you want to connect to the database from, so pick "My Local Environment".
Next is the "Add entries to your IP Access List" section. Fill in IP Address and Description as follows and submit:
means "all IPs can connect to the database". After adding, click "Finish and Close" to enter the database screen.
By default MongoDB auto-adds your current computer's IP address when you create a database. Setting 0.0.0.0 is for practice convenience — don't do this in production.
On the Overview page you'll see three ways to add data:
For convenience let's pick "Load Sample Data" — this fills in some sample data. The loading takes a moment. Once done, you'll see the Browse Collections button. Click it to see the database content.
Once you see the screen above, the MongoDB Atlas setup is done. Now let's set up the connection.
The official "MongoDB Compass" tool can connect you to MongoDB Atlas, but I won't cover that — I'll use Mongoose to connect to MongoDB Atlas directly.
If you want to try MongoDB Compass yourself, see my post
(28) Frontend dev forced to eat mango - Atlas - MongoDB Compass tutorial (article in Mandarin)
Run these commands to create a new project:
After install, let's pause and get familiar with what Mongoose is.
In short, Mongoose is a Node.js ODM (Object Data Modeling) that helps us operate MongoDB databases more easily in Node.js.
You can, but usually we don't operate MongoDB directly — we go through an ODM. Using an ODM lowers dev and maintenance costs. Unless you're really familiar with MongoDB, I'd recommend using an ODM.
What's an ODM? ODM stands for Object-Document Mapping. It maps database documents to objects, so you can operate the database through objects.
The other one is ORM - Object-Relational Mapping, used for relational databases like MySQL, PostgreSQL, etc.
In short, Mongoose integrates MongoDB operations for you, so you can use its wrapped methods to operate the database without writing native MongoDB commands.

OK there's one issue here — my
string doesn't apply to you, since everyone has their own connection string. How do you get yours? Go to the MongoDB Atlas page and click "Connect":
Scroll down and you'll see a connection string — copy it and paste into
What does this connection string look like? Don't worry, let me break it down.
For my example, the connection string looks like:
This connection string is made up of several parts. Let me break them down:
: the MongoDB Atlas connection string prefix.
indicates using MongoDB's SRV connection format. If you're using MongoDB's old connection format, this would be
: your MongoDB Atlas address — replace with yours.
means MongoDB Atlas auto-retries on write failure.
means MongoDB Atlas waits for the majority of database nodes to write successfully before responding.
So when connecting to MongoDB Atlas, just replace these parts with your data.
MongoDB connection strings have many more parameters — I won't cover all of them. For more, see
